behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is algorithm-quality verification of the heuristic itself (route length ratio vs optimal) and does not require real network/OS/subprocess or external side-effect readback. The failure signal is inside deterministic computation, so higher-level infrastructure adds little new reality signal.
behaviors.test_description: Run deterministic algorithm tests on many stop sets (n<=20), compute exact optimal route length (e.g., Held-Karp/brute force for bounded sizes), run heuristic, and assert heuristic_length/optimal_length <= 1.5.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is a deterministic formula/data-mapping check (distance + speed config -> time estimate). No explicit external infra contract, subprocess, or model output dependence is required.
behaviors.test_description: For representative route segments and road types, provide known distances and speed config values, then assert computed segment and total time windows match expected math (including rounding rules).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is pure validator logic on provided totals and shift-hours inputs, with deterministic reject/accept and excess calculation. No multi-module infra dependency is necessary to expose the core failure.
behaviors.test_description: Feed validator cases where total_estimated_time is below, equal to, and above remaining shift hours; assert rejection behavior and exact excess-time value for over-limit cases.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears in module handoff/orchestration: geocoding result handling -> filtered stop set -> optimizer input -> skipped-address reporting. A stubbed geocoder returning "no results" is sufficient to expose this without requiring real HTTP contract checks. Rejected higher level: no explicit requirement to verify real external response contract or real round-trip external side effect.
behaviors.test_description: Execute service flow with an in-process geocoder stub that returns coordinates for N-1 addresses and no-result for one address; assert the final optimized route excludes the failed address, includes all successful ones, and skipped-address report contains exactly that failed input.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: The behavior spans preprocessing/state handoff and routing output: deduplication + item merge must be preserved through optimization so the route visits once. This is beyond a single function check if you must prove end-to-end service behavior, but does not require real OS/network/subprocess or model output.
behaviors.test_description: Run service with duplicated address entries carrying different delivery items; assert output route contains one stop for that address, merged item payload is complete, and visit sequence includes the address exactly once.
